sources.notes: Read the full hard-version statement and constraints: n<=400, dense m up to n(n-1)/2, sum n<=400, and sum q<=3*10^5; unlike E1 there is no m<=400 sum bound.

Official E2 implementation confirms the hard optimization: process sorted edges with DSU, retain only component-merging edges, apply at most n-1 O(n^2) APSP transitions, and binary-search stored states.
Consulted for the direct proof that an edge whose endpoints already have zero distance cannot change APSP, so only n-1 component-merging updates execute, and for direct answer-table materialization.
Independent E1/E2 explanation cross-checking threshold 0/1 modeling and the at-most-n useful-update argument for the hard version.
Independent writeup explicitly identifies the useful zero-edge insertions as the n-1 DSU changes after contracting current zero components.
Inspected accepted E2 snapshot implementation: skips already zero-connected edges, stores only useful APSP states and their weights, then binary-searches queries.

Inspected materially distinct accepted E2 implementation: tests G[u][v]==0 instead of an explicit DSU and writes ans[a][b][new_distance+1]=w whenever a useful edge decreases a pair distance, giving O(1) queries.
Independently proved threshold equivalence, the one-edge recurrence, and zero-component skipping; compared full and DSU-skipped incremental APSP against recomputation on 480 random connected graphs.

reference_candidates.notes: Additional Luogu solution index; the concrete consulted article is listed in sources.

annotation.summary: Hard version: answer the same minimum kth-largest-edge path queries as E1, but m may be dense; only sum n<=400 remains small.
annotation.constraints: n<=400, n-1<=m<=n(n-1)/2, q<=3*10^5; sum n<=400 and sum q<=3*10^5, with no E1-style bound on total m. O(m n^2) is therefore too slow.
annotation.core_idea: Use E1's threshold model: at threshold X, edges w<=X cost 0 and heavier edges cost 1, and the answer is the first X with d_X(a,b)<k. The hard step is to avoid updating APSP for every edge. Sweep edges by weight with DSU over the current zero-cost edges. If u and v are already in the same zero component, a new zero edge (u,v) cannot improve any shortest path: replace every use of it by the existing zero-cost u-v path. Only an edge joining two zero components can matter; there are at most n-1 such edges, exactly the edges selected by a Kruskal sweep. For each useful edge apply d'(i,j)=min(d(i,j),d(i,u)+d(v,j),d(i,v)+d(u,j)) in O(n^2). Store the at most n APSP states and binary-search queries, or record the threshold directly whenever a pair distance drops.
annotation.complexity: O(n^3+m log m+q log n) time and O(n^3+m) memory for snapshots; the direct answer-table variant gives O(n^3+m log m+q) time with the same asymptotic memory.
tricks: Use the same kth-largest to thresholded 0/1 shortest-path reduction as E1.
A zero edge internal to a zero-connected component is distance-redundant for every vertex pair.
The only useful insertions are Kruskal/DSU merges, hence at most n-1.
Apply an O(n^2) incremental all-pairs relaxation only on useful edges.
Either store useful snapshots for binary search or materialize each (a,b,k) answer when its distance crosses k.

solution_variants.name: hard-kruskal-apsp-snapshots
solution_variants.summary: Official hard solution: sort edges, use DSU to keep only Kruskal merge edges, store the APSP matrix after each of at most n-1 useful zero-edge insertions, and binary-search each query.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n^3+m log m+q log n) time, O(n^3+m) memory.

solution_variants.name: hard-distance-drop-answer-table
solution_variants.summary: Use the current condition d(u,v)==0 as the zero-component test. For every useful edge, update each pair; a changed distance falls by exactly one because one existing cost-1 edge became cost 0, so write answer[a][b][new_distance+1]=w immediately. Queries are then O(1).
solution_variants.complexity: O(n^3+m log m+q) time, O(n^3+m) memory.

tags.evidence: The kth-order path objective is answered through all-pairs shortest counts of edges above each threshold in a 0/1 graph.
Initial APSP and each of the at most n-1 useful one-edge APSP updates use Floyd-Warshall-style min-plus relaxations.
tags.description: Use Floyd-Warshall all-pairs shortest paths, including Floyd-style O(n^2) relaxations after inserting one nonnegative edge, as the central engine on a small graph.
tags.created_reason: The existing graph/shortest-path tag is too broad to retrieve small-n APSP and incremental-Floyd problems such as both CF2057E versions. Main-agent taxonomy review is required before creating this candidate.

tags.evidence: DSU detects whether a threshold edge merges two zero components; only those at most n-1 merges trigger the expensive O(n^2) update.
The hard optimization is a Kruskal sweep: the retained component-merging edges form a minimum spanning tree and preserve every threshold's zero-component partition.
The proof for skipping a processed edge depends exactly on whether its endpoints already have a zero-cost connecting path.
Threshold distances only decrease, enabling first-feasible-state search and one-time answer materialization when a distance crosses k.
The official snapshot variant binary-searches at most n monotone useful states for each query.
Edges must be swept in nondecreasing weight so DSU merges and stored thresholds follow candidate answer order.
The graph is preprocessed across all sorted weight thresholds before answering the large query batch.
